hero.title: I’m fascinated by where the logic of software meets everyday life.
hero.description: I’m motivated by turning complex ideas into clear and useful experiences. I don’t see interfaces as isolated elements, but as part of a system where each component has a purpose and contributes to a coherent experience.
work.label: Selected projects
projects.baseball.title: Baseball Scoreboard
projects.baseball.description: The important decision here wasn’t visual, it was architectural: synchronizing the operator console and projection screen on the client without relying on a server. I designed the interface, wrote the code, and used it in production during the Ministerio Cristiano HOME tournament.
projects.nexo.title: Nexo
projects.nexo.description: New project added automatically. Details in development.
about.title: I understand the system before designing the screen.
about.paragraph0: I don’t follow a rigid process. I often understand the problem while I’m designing: I look at how other products solve similar challenges, even in completely different contexts, and explore which principles can be transferred. I first identify what works, then I look for the friction points.
about.paragraph1: My background in Software Engineering at Universidad APEC directly influences how I design. I think in systems, behaviors, and unexpected scenarios. I’m interested not only in how an experience looks, but in how it works when people interact with it.
about.paragraph2: For me, design and development are part of the same conversation. The best experiences emerge when design decisions consider both user needs and the technical reality of the product.
about.paragraph3: I value standards because they bring clarity and consistency, but I also believe each product should build its own identity and a unique way of relating to the people who use it.
about.disciplines.title: Disciplines
about.stack.title: Stack
about.learning.title: Familiar / Learning
about.education.title: Education
about.philosophy.title: Philosophy
about.philosophy.item1: Understand before designing. I first identify how a process works, where friction appears, and what people truly need before proposing solutions.
about.philosophy.item2: Think in systems. I don’t see interfaces as isolated elements. Each screen is part of a larger system with rules, states, and dependencies.
about.philosophy.item3: Design and technology are the same conversation. The best experiences emerge when design decisions consider both user needs and the technical reality of the product.

case.compare.heading: Before and after
case.screens.heading: Main screens
case.decisions.heading: Design decisions
case.learnings.heading: What I take away
case.cta.title: Have a project?
case.cta.subtitle: Let's talk.
case.nexo.hero.subtitle: Mobile platform to centralize the management of essential public services: checking status, paying, reporting incidents, and tracking — all in one place.
case.nexo.problem.heading: Scattered services, requests with no follow-up
case.nexo.problem.body: Each public service (water, electricity, among others) lives in its own channel: different apps, different offices, different processes. Citizens have no single place to check their services, pay, or follow up on a report, and once they report an issue, they lose all visibility into its status.
case.nexo.problem.goal1: Unify access to the different services in one place
case.nexo.problem.goal2: Reduce the steps to pay and report an incident
case.nexo.problem.goal3: Give visible tracking to the status of each request
case.nexo.compare.before1: Each service with its own channel, with no shared visibility.
case.nexo.compare.before2: Reporting an issue generated no follow-up afterward.
case.nexo.compare.before3: Paying required leaving the app for another channel.
case.nexo.compare.after1: A single dashboard with all the citizen's services.
case.nexo.compare.after2: Every report generates a case with status and notifications.
case.nexo.compare.after3: Payment integrated into the same flow, without leaving the app.
case.nexo.process.heading: Design process
case.nexo.process.step1.title: Information architecture
case.nexo.process.step1.body: I defined how services, payments, incidents, and notifications are grouped so the user understands the status of each request at a glance, mapping the relationship between account, service, and case.
case.nexo.process.step2.title: User flows
case.nexo.process.step2.body: I designed the main flows — checking a service, paying, reporting an incident, and tracking status — prioritizing fewer screens for the most frequent tasks.
case.nexo.process.step3.title: Design system
case.nexo.process.step3.body: I built a component system (service cards, status badges, report forms) reusable across different services without losing visual consistency.
case.nexo.process.step4.title: UI
case.nexo.process.step4.body: I applied the system to the final screens, taking care of visual hierarchy, empty and error states, and contrast/tap-target accessibility.
case.nexo.decisions.card1.title: Service card as the base unit
case.nexo.decisions.card1.body: Each service is represented as a card with a visible status (up to date, pending, with an incident), letting you scan the whole dashboard at a glance.
case.nexo.decisions.card2.title: One single report flow for every service
case.nexo.decisions.card2.body: Instead of a separate form per service type, one flow with conditional fields based on the selected service, reducing cognitive load.
case.nexo.decisions.card3.title: Request status always visible
case.nexo.decisions.card3.body: Every payment or report is tied to a status (received, in progress, resolved) visible right from the main dashboard, with no need to search another section.
case.nexo.results.heading: From scattered requests to a single place
case.nexo.results.stat1.label: Single dashboard for all of a citizen's services
case.nexo.results.stat2.label: Steps to report an incident, down from 6
case.nexo.results.stat3.label: Of requests with visible status and tracking in the app
case.nexo.learnings.item1.title: Designing for consistency across different services
case.nexo.learnings.item1.body: The app had to serve services with different logics without the user feeling like they were switching products in every section. This reinforced the importance of a flexible yet stable design system.
case.nexo.learnings.item2.title: Status is the most important piece of information
case.nexo.learnings.item2.body: In a service-management product, what users need to see most is the status of their request. That principle guided much of the information architecture and UI decisions.
case.baseball.hero.eyebrow: Case Study · Product in real use
case.baseball.hero.subtitle: I turned a manual, error-prone process operated under pressure into a digital tool that synchronizes the operator console with the public projection screen in real time for a Bible Baseball tournament.
case.baseball.hero.typeValue: Digital product · Real use in tournament
case.baseball.context.heading: From the physical whiteboard to software
case.baseball.context.p1: Every year, Ministerio Cristiano HOME organizes a community Bible Baseball tournament. For several editions, the scoreboard was kept on physical whiteboards and PowerPoint slides, updated by hand by an operator.
case.baseball.context.p2: I was that operator. As a volunteer, I saw firsthand how much the outcome depended on one person's attention: a forgotten out, a miscounted run, or an out-of-sync slide could break the whole audience's experience in seconds.
case.baseball.context.p3: That friction, repeated tournament after tournament, is what turned a volunteer task into a design problem I wanted to solve. The project started that way, as a personal initiative, and evolved together with the organizers into the system the tournament uses today.
case.baseball.role.heading: Design and development, end to end
case.baseball.role.body: I took part in every stage of the project: field research, information architecture, experience and interface design, prototyping, frontend development, game-logic implementation, and the iterations that followed real use.
case.baseball.role.goal1: Researching the process and gathering the tournament's rules
case.baseball.role.goal3: Visual design, prototyping, and frontend development
case.baseball.role.goal4: Syncing views and continuous iteration after real use
case.baseball.users.heading: Four roles, one single source of truth
case.baseball.users.body: The system had to serve people with very different needs within the same event, all synced around the same scoreboard.
case.baseball.users.card1.title: Operator
case.baseball.users.card1.body: Records every play in real time. Needs to work fast and make as few mistakes as possible.
case.baseball.users.card2.title: Coordinator
case.baseball.users.card2.body: Registers the teams and batting order before each game starts.
case.baseball.users.card3.title: Projection team
case.baseball.users.card3.body: Controls the screen the audience sees, without touching the game record.
case.baseball.users.card4.title: Audience
case.baseball.users.card4.body: Views the scoreboard from the big screen and also from their own mobile devices.
case.baseball.problem.heading: Operating a live tournament, under pressure
case.baseball.problem.body: The challenge wasn't showing a scoreboard. It was designing for an operator recording plays in real time, with no room to pause the game or calmly correct mistakes.
case.baseball.problem.goal1: Avoid human error while recording plays
case.baseball.problem.goal4: Adapt to Bible Baseball's own rules
case.baseball.compare.before1: Physical whiteboards and PowerPoint slides updated by hand by a single operator.
case.baseball.compare.before2: Frequent errors recalculating the live scoreboard, with no way to undo them.
case.baseball.compare.before3: No sync at all between what the operator recorded and what the audience saw.
case.baseball.compare.after1: Digital recording: the most frequent actions run with a single click.
case.baseball.compare.after2: Automatic sync between the operator console and the projection screen.
case.baseball.compare.after3: Works completely offline, with Bible Baseball's rules built in.
case.baseball.research.heading: Understanding the domain before designing any screen
case.baseball.research.p1: Before touching a single interface, I watched several tournaments, worked as an operator myself, documented the rules, and asked the organizers about everything I didn't know. Domain knowledge — the game's pace, Bible Baseball's own rules, the operator's real pressure — was essential before designing anything.
case.baseball.research.p2: That fieldwork surfaced the most common errors the tool had to prevent:
case.baseball.research.goal5: Losing sync with the projection screen
case.baseball.research.p3: I also noticed most in-game actions were repetitive. That observation became the guiding principle for the whole design: every frequent action had to be resolved with as few clicks as possible.
case.baseball.principles.heading: Rules that guided every decision
case.baseball.principles.item3: Prevent errors rather than fix them
case.baseball.principles.item4: Show only the information that's needed
case.baseball.principles.item8: Make the system learnable in a few minutes
case.baseball.process.heading: Four iterations, validated on the field
case.baseball.process.step1.title: Sketches over the existing solution
case.baseball.process.step1.body: I started with quick sketches using the PowerPoint that was in use as a reference, to identify what information was essential to keep.
case.baseball.process.step2.title: Functional prototype in Figma
case.baseball.process.step2.body: I used Figma Make to speed up building a first functional prototype, which let me quickly validate the interface layout and better understand the real operating flow.
case.baseball.process.step3.title: AI-assisted iteration
case.baseball.process.step3.body: From that base, I iterated with the help of AI tools to explore interface alternatives, refine components, review flows, and catch edge cases. The final product, experience, and interface decisions were always mine, validated during real use of the system.
case.baseball.process.step4.title: Validation in a real tournament
case.baseball.process.step4.body: I used the system myself as an operator in real games. That real use uncovered new rules and kept the product improving between tournament editions.
case.baseball.decisions.card1.title: Operator console
case.baseball.decisions.card1.body: Designed for speed and precision: the most frequent actions stay always visible, while rarely used settings stay hidden until needed.
case.baseball.decisions.card2.title: Projection screen
case.baseball.decisions.card2.body: It's not a copy of the console. It was designed exclusively for the audience, prioritizing legibility from a distance and showing only the essential scoreboard information.
case.baseball.decisions.card3.title: One-click recording
case.baseball.decisions.card3.body: Actions that happen dozens of times per game — an out, a run, a turn change — run with a single action, no intermediate steps.
case.baseball.decisions.card4.title: Error prevention
case.baseball.decisions.card4.body: Separated actions, clear visual states, and a play history let you undo and recover information without interrupting the game.
case.baseball.decisions.card5.title: Dark mode
case.baseball.decisions.card5.body: It wasn't an aesthetic choice. It reduces the operator's eye strain during days with several consecutive games.
case.baseball.implementation.heading: How it works under the hood
case.baseball.implementation.p1: Built with React, TypeScript, and Vite, with Framer Motion for interface transitions.
case.baseball.implementation.p2: The operator console and the projection screen sync via localStorage and StorageEvent, which lets the system work completely offline — a non-negotiable requirement at the venue where the tournament is played.
case.baseball.implementation.p3: The game logic (counting outs, runs, innings, and Bible Baseball's special rules) was implemented as pure functions, separate from the interface, so they could be tested and adjusted in isolation every time a new rule came up.
case.baseball.results.heading: A system proven game after game, in a real tournament
case.baseball.results.stat1.label: Games operated with the system
case.baseball.results.stat2.label: Teams competing in the tournament
case.baseball.results.stat3.label: In-person spectators per day
case.baseball.results.note: The solution worked throughout the whole tournament, and in the process, new rules came to light that keep feeding product iterations. The system will be used again in future editions.
case.baseball.learnings.item1.title: Understanding the domain before designing
case.baseball.learnings.item1.body: Bible Baseball's rules and a game's real pace weren't documented anywhere. Without that fieldwork, any interface would have solved the wrong problem.
case.baseball.learnings.item2.title: Designing for users under pressure
case.baseball.learnings.item2.body: An operator mid-game has no time to think about the interface. Every design decision had to be weighed against that pressure, not against an ideal-use scenario.
case.baseball.learnings.item3.title: Validating in real scenarios
case.baseball.learnings.item3.body: No prototype revealed as much as operating the system myself during a real tournament, game after game.
case.baseball.learnings.item4.title: Turning complex rules into simple experiences
case.baseball.learnings.item4.body: Bible Baseball's special rules kept adding edge cases. The design challenge was absorbing that complexity without passing it on to the operator.
case.baseball.learnings.item5.title: AI as an accelerator, not a replacement for judgment
case.baseball.learnings.item5.body: I used AI to explore alternatives and speed up implementation, but the product, experience, and interface decisions remained mine, validated with real use.

agent.faqAnswer.who: I'm Gregory Durán, a Product Designer with a background in Software Engineering.
agent.faqAnswer.what: I design digital products and focus on understanding the whole problem before thinking about a visual solution.
agent.faqAnswer.projects: I've worked on projects like Baseball Scoreboard, Jobs Hunter, Pulse, and HidroCity.
agent.faqAnswer.skills: I have experience in product design, UX/UI, research, information architecture, and frontend development with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, and React.
agent.faqAnswer.contact: You can write to me at gregorymduran01@outlook.com.
agent.faqAnswer.process: I don't follow a fixed methodology. Sometimes I start by writing ideas, other times with mental diagrams, or designing directly depending on the project's context.
